title: Category 3 Sewage Cleanup — PPE, Containment, Disposal & Hygienist Clearance for Australian Restorers
description: A complete guide to Category 3 sewage backup cleanup in Australia — required PPE, containment procedures, material disposal, antimicrobial treatment, and hygienist clearance. Covers IICRC S500:2025 §7.3 requirements and Australian WHS obligations.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## Category 3: Grossly Contaminated Water

Category 3, also known as black water, is the highest contamination level defined in IICRC S500:2025. Sources include sewage backflow from sewer systems, flooding from rivers and storm drains, and water that has contacted raw sewage or significant quantities of soil and organic matter. The contamination may include pathogenic bacteria (E. coli, Salmonella), viruses (hepatitis A, norovirus), and other biological hazards.

In Australia, sewage backup claims are common after significant rainfall events that overwhelm municipal sewer systems, as well as from blocked private drain lines. They represent the highest risk category for technician health and safety and carry the most stringent scope requirements.

## PPE Requirements

Under Australian WHS regulations and IICRC S500:2025, the minimum PPE for Category 3 work includes:

- **Respiratory protection**: P2/P100 particulate respirator at minimum; P3 with organic vapour cartridges recommended for active sewage cleanup due to hydrogen sulphide and volatile organic compound risks
- **Eye protection**: Sealed chemical splash goggles (not safety glasses, which leave gaps around the eyes)
- **Gloves**: Chemical-resistant nitrile or similar; doubled gloving recommended when handling actively contaminated materials
- **Body protection**: Disposable Tyvek coveralls (Type 5/6) to prevent skin contact and cross-contamination; booties over boots
- **Decontamination procedure**: Technicians must undergo a decontamination procedure (wash-down or change of coveralls) before leaving the work area

PPE must be documented in the job record. Under Australian WHS law, the PCBU (the restoration company) must provide appropriate PPE and ensure its correct use.

## Containment Requirements

Containment prevents cross-contamination of Category 3 materials into unaffected areas of the building. For sewage work, containment should include:

- Physical barriers (poly sheeting) at all openings from the affected area
- Negative air pressure maintained inside containment using air scrubbers with HEPA filtration
- A decontamination airlock for technician entry and exit
- Signage restricting access to authorised personnel only

## Material Removal: IICRC S500:2025 §7.3

Under §7.3 of IICRC S500:2025, all porous materials that have been contacted by Category 3 water must be removed and disposed of. There is no drying-in-place option for porous materials in Category 3 scenarios. This includes:

- Carpet and underlay
- Plasterboard (gypsum board) to a minimum 150–300 mm above the waterline
- Insulation (batts or blown)
- Cardboard, paper, and other highly porous items

Semi-porous materials (timber framing, concrete, ceramic tile) that are structurally sound may be cleaned, disinfected with an EPA-registered antimicrobial product, and retained — provided the surface is accessible for effective treatment.

## Disposal Requirements

All Category 3 waste must be double-bagged in heavy-duty polyethylene bags (minimum 200 µm) and sealed. In most Australian jurisdictions, Category 3 waste is classified as putrescible waste and must be disposed of at an approved facility. Check with your local council and waste authority for facility locations and disposal requirements. Some councils require prior notification for large volumes of putrescible waste.

## Hygienist Clearance

For Category 3 jobs exceeding minor sewage cleanup (typically >1 m²), post-remediation clearance testing by a qualified hygienist is recommended and may be required by the insurer. The hygienist will collect surface swabs and/or air samples to confirm that contamination levels are within acceptable limits before the area is cleared for reoccupation.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're covering Category 3 sewage cleanup — the most hazardous work category in residential restoration, and one where getting the methodology wrong has serious consequences for health, liability, and insurance payment.

Let's start with who should be doing this work. Category 3 is grossly contaminated water — sewage backflow, flooding from drains and rivers, any water with significant faecal matter or soil contamination. The biological hazards are real: E. coli, Salmonella, hepatitis A, norovirus. This is not a job for anyone without appropriate training and PPE.

Minimum PPE for Category 3: P2 particulate respirator at minimum — P3 with organic vapour cartridges is better if there's active sewage present due to hydrogen sulphide. Sealed chemical splash goggles, not just safety glasses. Chemical-resistant nitrile gloves, doubled. Disposable Tyvek coveralls, Type 5 or 6, with booties. And a decontamination procedure before leaving the work area — change your coveralls in the decon zone, not in the clean zone.

Containment. You need physical poly barriers at all openings, negative air pressure inside containment using HEPA-filtered air scrubbers, and a decontamination airlock. Access restriction signage is required. The goal is to keep contaminated particulates inside the work area.

Material removal. IICRC S500:2025 section 7.3 is very clear: all porous materials contacted by Category 3 water must be removed. No exceptions, no drying in place. Carpet and underlay, plasterboard to at least 150 to 300 millimetres above the waterline, insulation, cardboard — all out. Semi-porous materials like timber framing or ceramic tile that are structurally sound can be cleaned and treated with an EPA-registered antimicrobial.

All Category 3 waste goes into double heavy-duty poly bags, sealed, and disposed of at an approved facility. In most Australian jurisdictions this is putrescible waste — check your local council requirements.

For anything more than minor sewage cleanup, get a hygienist in for post-remediation clearance testing before the area is cleared for reoccupation. Surface swabs and air samples confirm the contamination has been adequately addressed. Some insurers require this as a condition of settlement.

In RestoreAssist, selecting Category 3 on the inspection automatically flags all porous material line items for removal in the scope, and the PPE checklist is included in the inspection workflow.

Head to restoreassist.com.au.
